name: Guinness Draught
description: Guinness Draught is the definitive dry Irish stout and one of the most recognizable drinks in the world: near-black with garnet edges, capped by a dense ivory head produced not by carbonation alone but by nitrogen. The nitro serve — tiny, stable bubbles from a restrictor faucet or the in-can 'widget' — gives a famously creamy mouthfeel to what is actually a light, sessionable 4.2% beer. Flavor comes from roasted unmalted barley: dry coffee and dark-chocolate bitterness over a lightly sweet malt middle, finishing clean and moreish. Brewed at St. James's Gate in Dublin since the Georgian era (and under license worldwide), it is less a craft object than a cultural institution, complete with its own two-part pouring ritual and the harp that doubles as a national symbol.
history: Arthur Guinness signed a famous 9,000-year lease on the St. James's Gate brewery in 1759 at £45 a year, moving from ale to porter and then to the stouter export versions that conquered the world. The harp trademark dates to 1876. The modern Draught product was born in 1959, when brewer Michael Ash's nitrogenation system solved the problem of replicating cask creaminess; the widget brought the nitro pour to cans in 1989 and later to bottles.
production: A grist of pale malt, flaked barley and roasted unmalted barley — the source of the color and dry, coffee-like bite — is brewed with bittering hops and fermented with the house's centuries-old yeast strain. The beer is filtered, then dispensed or packaged with a roughly 75/25 nitrogen-to-carbon-dioxide mix; nitrogen's insolubility creates the cascading surge and tight, long-lasting head that define the pour.
coloring: No added coloring — color from roasted barley

tastingNotes.nose: Roasted barley, coffee and dark cocoa with a faint bready sweetness
tastingNotes.palate: Creamy, light-bodied and smooth; milk chocolate and espresso over gentle malt sweetness
tastingNotes.finish: Dry, roasty and clean with a softly bitter coffee close

servingTemp: About 6 °C, via the two-part pour: fill three-quarters, let it settle, then top up
facts: Arthur Guinness's 1759 lease on St. James's Gate runs for 9,000 years at £45 per annum.
The Guinness harp, trademarked in 1876, faces the opposite direction from the Irish state's official harp emblem so the two could coexist.
Marketing canonized a 119.5-second 'perfect pour'; the surge is nitrogen physics, not carbonation.
The 1989 widget — a nitrogen-releasing capsule in the can — won the Queen's Award for Technological Achievement in 1991.

timeline.event: Arthur Guinness leases St. James's Gate for 9,000 years
Nitrogenated Guinness Draught launched for the bicentenary
Widget brings the nitro pour to cans

name: Sierra Nevada Pale Ale
description: Sierra Nevada Pale Ale is the beer that defined the American pale ale and, by extension, lit the fuse of the craft-beer revolution. First brewed by Ken Grossman in Chico, California in November 1980, it took the balanced English pale-ale template and re-voiced it with a single audacious choice: generous whole-cone Cascade hops, whose piney, grapefruit character had never anchored a commercial flagship before. Against the bland lagers of its era it tasted radical; today its blueprint — caramel-tinged malt backbone, assertive but drinkable American hop finish, 5.6% strength — reads as the genre's textbook. Still brewed with whole-cone hops and still bottle-conditioned with a dab of yeast for freshness, it remains one of the best-selling craft beers in the United States more than four decades on.
history: Ken Grossman, a homebrew-shop owner, and co-founder Paul Camusi built the original brewery by hand in 1980 from scrapped dairy tanks and salvaged equipment, naming it for Grossman's beloved Sierra Nevada mountains. The pale ale was among its first beers and became its engine, funding growth into one of America's largest family-owned breweries, with a second brewery in Mills River, North Carolina opening in 2014 and a reputation for obsessive sustainability engineering.
production: A grist of two-row pale and caramel malts provides the amber color and toasty-sweet backbone. Magnum hops handle bittering, with Cascade — used exclusively in whole-cone form, never pellets — layered late in the boil and in the fermenter's aftermath for the signature pine-and-grapefruit aroma. Fermentation uses the house ale yeast, and the packaged beer is bottle-conditioned: refermented with a small yeast addition for natural carbonation and shelf stability.

tastingNotes.nose: Grapefruit peel and pine resin over toasted caramel malt and a floral, spicy hop note
tastingNotes.palate: Medium-bodied and balanced; bready caramel sweetness meeting brisk citrus-pine hop bitterness
tastingNotes.finish: Crisp and moderately bitter, grapefruit pith fading into clean malt

servingTemp: 7–10 °C; drink fresh for peak hop aroma
facts: Ken Grossman welded the first brewhouse together largely from discarded dairy equipment in 1980.
Sierra Nevada is one of the few large breweries that still insists on whole-cone hops rather than pellets.
Cascade, the hop that defines this beer, was only released by the USDA breeding program in 1972 — eight years before the beer that made it famous.

timeline.event: First batch of Sierra Nevada Pale Ale brewed in Chico, California
Second brewery opens in Mills River, North Carolina

name: Chimay Grande Réserve (Blue)
description: Chimay Blue is the strong dark flagship of the Abbey of Notre-Dame de Scourmont and, for much of the world, the first authentic Trappist beer within arm's reach. Born in 1948 as a Christmas beer and later made year-round (the 750 ml corked format carries the Grande Réserve name), it pours deep chestnut under a rocky tan head: fresh bread and caramel, plum and dried fig, and the peppery, faintly smoky spice of the abbey's own yeast strain, all wrapped around a warming 9% that stays surprisingly nimble. Bottle-conditioned and vintage-dated on the cap, it ages gracefully for years, drying out and gaining port-like nuance. Widely exported yet still brewed within the monastery walls under monastic oversight, it made 'Trappist' a style cue as much as a legal designation.
history: Monks from Westvleteren founded Scourmont Abbey near Chimay in 1850 and began brewing in 1862 — the first brewery to sell beer under the Trappist name. After WWII, Father Théodore De Haene isolated the abbey's house yeast strain in 1948 with guidance from Leuven brewing scientist Jean De Clerck, creating the modern Chimay range beginning with this dark Christmas ale. De Clerck is buried at the abbey. Profits fund the monastic community and regional charities.
production: Brewed inside the abbey with water from its own wells: pale and caramelized malts plus wheat starch, dark candi sugar for strength and color, and continental hops for restrained bitterness. The 1948 house yeast drives a warm fermentation rich in dark-fruit esters and spicy phenols. The beer is dosed with fresh yeast and sugar at packaging and refermented in the bottle, with caps and corks printed with the year for cellaring.
coloring: No added coloring — color from dark malts and candi sugar

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh-baked brown bread, plum and fig with caramel, clove and a peppery yeast note
tastingNotes.palate: Full, smooth and lively; dark fruit and toffee balanced by spicy phenols and gentle roast
tastingNotes.finish: Long and warming, drying to cocoa, raisin and a whisper of pepper

servingTemp: 10–12 °C in a chalice, poured slowly; vintage bottles cellar for 5+ years
facts: Chimay Blue began life in 1948 as 'Spéciale Noël', a Christmas beer, before going year-round.
Every cap and cork is printed with the bottling year — the brewery openly encourages cellaring.
Brewing scientist Jean De Clerck, who helped Father Théodore isolate the house yeast in 1948, chose to be buried at Scourmont Abbey.

timeline.event: Brewing begins at Scourmont Abbey — the first beer sold as 'Trappist'
House yeast isolated; dark Christmas ale that becomes Chimay Blue first brewed
Corked 750 ml format takes the name Grande Réserve

name: Pliny the Elder
description: Pliny the Elder is the archetypal double IPA — the beer that proved an 8% hop bomb could be bone-dry, snappily bitter and dangerously drinkable rather than syrupy. Vinnie Cilurzo, who had brewed the style's very first commercial example at Blind Pig in 1994, created Pliny for the inaugural Double IPA Festival in 2000, and it went on to spend years atop American best-beer polls. Pine resin, grapefruit and dank herbal hops dominate a deliberately stripped-back malt frame; the label's string of bottled-on dates and slogans ('Respect your elders... keep it cold, drink it fresh') enforce the freshness doctrine the beer helped canonize. Distribution remains deliberately thin — a handful of states, no contract brewing — which keeps queues forming wherever it lands.
history: Russian River Brewing was founded in 1997 by Korbel, the champagne-cellars company, and sold in 2003 to its brewer Vinnie Cilurzo and his wife Natalie, who opened the Santa Rosa brewpub in 2004 and a larger Windsor brewery in 2018. Pliny the Elder honors the Roman naturalist tied by legend to the naming of the hop plant; its even scarcer sibling, the triple IPA Pliny the Younger, causes multi-hour lines each February.
production: A pale grist with minimal crystal malt and a dose of sugar dries the body out so hops can lead. Amarillo, Centennial, CTZ and Simcoe supply bittering, flavor and heavy dry-hop additions — layered for pine, citrus and resinous dankness at around 100 IBU. Fermented clean with a Californian ale yeast, packaged unpasteurized in the signature 510 ml bottle, and aggressively date-stamped because hop aromatics fade within weeks.
filtration: Filtered; unpasteurized — meant to be drunk fresh

tastingNotes.nose: Pine resin, grapefruit zest and dank herbal hops over faint honeyed malt
tastingNotes.palate: Dry and vivid; resinous bitterness, tangerine and pine needle on a lean, clean body that hides its 8%
tastingNotes.finish: Long, sharply bitter and clean — pith and pine with no cloying sweetness

servingTemp: 7–8 °C, as fresh as you can find it — check the bottling date
facts: Vinnie Cilurzo brewed the first commercial double IPA at Blind Pig Brewing in Temecula in 1994, six years before creating Pliny.
The beer debuted at the Bistro's inaugural Double IPA Festival in Hayward, California in 2000.
Bottles carry the motto 'Respect your elders' alongside multiple freshness dates — the brewery would rather you not cellar it.
Its February-only sibling Pliny the Younger draws hours-long lines and hotel-booking spikes in Sonoma County.

timeline.event: Pliny the Elder debuts at the inaugural Double IPA Festival
Vinnie and Natalie Cilurzo buy Russian River Brewing from Korbel
Windsor production brewery opens, easing (slightly) the chronic shortage

name: Orval
description: Orval is the eccentric of the Trappist family: a single beer, sold in a skittle-shaped bottle, that belongs to no tidy style category. Brewed since 1931 at the Abbey of Notre-Dame d'Orval in Belgium's Gaume region, it is dry-hopped in the English manner — rare in Belgium — and re-seeded at bottling with Brettanomyces, the wild yeast that slowly eats residual sugars and reshapes the beer in the bottle. Young Orval is bright, hoppy and peppery; a year on it turns drier, funkier and more leathery, which is why devotees date-check caps and argue endlessly about the ideal age. Amber-orange under a monumental rocky head, bone-dry and endlessly aromatic, it is a fixture of every 'greatest beers' conversation and the only beer its abbey makes for the public.
history: Orval Abbey traces to 1132; legend has Countess Matilda of Tuscany recovering her lost wedding ring from the abbey spring in a trout's mouth, exclaiming that the place was a golden valley — 'Val d'Or' — the trout-and-ring image still on every bottle. Destroyed after the French Revolution, the abbey was rebuilt from 1926, and the brewery was established in 1931 expressly to fund reconstruction. Anne-Françoise Pypaert became the first female master brewer in Trappist history there in 2013.
production: Pale and caramel malts with liquid candi sugar form a deceptively simple base, bittered and then generously dry-hopped with continental varieties. Primary fermentation uses the house ale yeast; at bottling the beer is dosed with fresh sugar and a mixed culture including Brettanomyces for months of secondary evolution. Bottle-conditioning drives the ABV from roughly 5.2% toward 6.2% or beyond as the brett works, drying the beer and layering in its hallmark horse-blanket funk.

tastingNotes.nose: Orange peel, hop sack and white pepper over leather, hay and gentle horse-blanket funk
tastingNotes.palate: Bone dry and effervescent; bitter orange, herbal hops and tart, earthy brett character on a lean frame
tastingNotes.finish: Long, dry and appetizing — peppery funk and hop bitterness that call for another sip

servingTemp: 12–14 °C in the house chalice; compare a fresh bottle against one aged a year
facts: The trout with a golden ring on the label comes from the founding legend of Countess Matilda's recovered wedding ring.
Orval is the only beer its abbey sells to the public — plus a small 'Petite Orval' reserved for the monks.
Brettanomyces added at bottling means every bottle is a slow-motion experiment; 'young versus old Orval' is a genuine schism among fans.
Anne-Françoise Pypaert became the first female Trappist master brewer when she took over Orval's brewhouse in 2013.

timeline.event: Brewery founded to finance the abbey's reconstruction; Orval first sold
Anne-Françoise Pypaert becomes the first female Trappist master brewer

name: Dassai 23 Junmai Daiginjo
description: Dassai 23 is modern sake's calling card: a junmai daiginjo whose name announces its extravagance — each grain of Yamada Nishiki rice is milled down to 23% of its original size, stripping away the fats and proteins that mask aroma until only the starchy heart remains. When it launched in 1992 that was the most extreme polishing ratio in commercial sake. The result is a genre-defining ginjo profile: honeydew melon, white peach and pear blossom over a delicately sweet, silky palate that finishes clean and faintly floral. Asahi Shuzo brews it with heretical modernity — no master brewer, year-round production, decisions driven by fermentation data — and sells it worldwide as deliberately accessible luxury, a sake meant for wine glasses as much as ochoko.
history: Asahi Shuzo was a struggling rural brewery in mountain Yamaguchi when Hiroshi Sakurai took over in 1984. Betting everything on premium junmai daiginjo, he launched Dassai in 1990 and the 23 in 1992, abolishing the seasonal toji (master brewer) system after their toji departed and replacing craft intuition with measurement and a salaried team brewing year-round. The gamble built one of Japan's most successful sake businesses, culminating in the Dassai Blue brewery in New York State in 2023.
production: Yamada Nishiki — the king of sake rice — is polished for about 168 hours to a 23% seimaibuai, washed and steamed in small batches. Koji is grown by hand in temperature-controlled rooms, and the multiple parallel fermentation proceeds cold and slow in small tanks inside a climate-controlled multi-storey kura, monitored with daily analytical data rather than a toji's rule of thumb. The sake is pressed gently, with no added distilled alcohol — junmai — and shipped refrigerated.
filtration: No activated-charcoal filtration; pressed and lightly fined

tastingNotes.nose: Honeydew melon, white peach and steamed rice with pear blossom and a hint of anise
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and gently sweet; melon and ripe pear over a fine acidity and a whisper of umami
tastingNotes.finish: Clean, cool and moderately long, closing on white flowers and wet stone

servingTemp: Chilled, 8–12 °C; this delicate daiginjo style is not suited to warming
facts: Dassai means 'otter festival', from the brewery's home hamlet of Osogoe ('otter crossing') and a poetic name for scattered manuscripts.
Asahi Shuzo has no toji: brewing decisions are made by salaried staff from fermentation data, year-round, in a climate-controlled tower kura.
At launch in 1992, 23% was the lowest rice-polishing ratio in commercial sake — the number was chosen to beat a rival's 24%.
Dassai Blue, the company's Hyde Park, New York brewery, opened in 2023 to brew sake in the US.

timeline.event: Asahi Shuzo founded in rural Yamaguchi
Dassai brand launched, all-in on junmai daiginjo
Dassai 23 debuts with a record 23% polishing ratio
Dassai Blue brewery opens in New York State
